1:
The chapter opens on a deathbed, and the witnesses read it as the solemn handing-over of a covenant trust. Matthew Henry sets the tone: David, "that great and good man," is here a dying man, and death "stains all the glory of this" life and lays it in the dust. Yet he hears David's own phrase — I go the way of all the earth — not as despair but as a doorway, for death is "not only a period of this life, but a passage to a better," and even "the sons and heirs of heaven must go the way of all the earth." John Gill grounds the moment in providence: David's days were "fixed and determined by the Lord," and their approach could be read in the growing infirmities of age. He passes on a rabbinic curiosity from Abarbinel — that the text names him only David, not King David, because Solomon was already anointed and reigning, or, as other Jews held, because "no man, even a king, has power in the day of death."

The grammatical-historical reading of Keil and Delitzsch fixes on the charge itself. David's first concern is not his throne but his son's obedience: "be strong and be a man" means not, as Thenius supposed, "bear my departure bravely," but "prove thyself brave to keep the commandments of the Lord." The idiom "to keep the keeping of Jehovah" — to guard whatever is to be guarded in relation to God — echoes the language of the Torah and takes its precise content from the context that follows. Death, David says, is "the way of all the world," the very words Joshua had used at his own farewell (Joshua 23:14).

Henry draws out the architecture of the charge that the whole chapter will unfold. David gives Solomon "a good rule to act by" — the divine will; "a good spirit to act with" — resolution, to "show thyself a man, though in years but a child"; and "good reasons," the prosperity and the perpetuity of his throne. The Messianic promise that a son of David should come was absolute; but the promise "that there should not fail him a man on the throne of Israel was conditional," hanging on obedience. Adam Clarke, surveying the whole chapter, lists its grim sequel — the charges concerning Joab and Shimei, the fall of Adonijah, the banishing of Abiathar to Anathoth — and so warns the reader that this tender farewell will issue in a season of blood. On the substance, though, the company is one: a dying father's authority "is much, but nothing to that of a living God," and the best thing he can bequeath his heir is to keep the charge of the Lord.
2:
The witnesses turn David's opening word — I go the way of all the earth — into a meditation on death as the great leveller, and then on the manhood it demands. John Gill reads the phrase as "a path which is the path of death, which all pass in, kings and peasants, high and low, rich and poor, great and small"; none are exempted, for it is "the appointment of God, a decree which can never be reversed." He notes that David seems to have borrowed the very words from Joshua's farewell (Joshua 23:14), a debt Charles Ellicott registers with a single cross-reference to the same passage.

Adam Clarke expands the thought into a full elegy. Death, he says, "is no respecter of persons; he visits the palace of the king as well as the cottage of the peasant," and he heaps up the pagan poets — Horace's pale Death knocking "with equal pace" at the poor man's hut and the king's tower — and Blair's Grave, to press home that emperors and beggars "meet in the same narrow house." Even the beasts share the necessity, for "it is the way of all the earth, both of men and inferior animals." Yet Clarke will not let the verse sink into mere mortality. On show thyself a man he turns sharply practical: "Act like a rational being, and not like a brute; and remember, that he acts most like a man who is most devoted to his God."

The grammatical-historical reading of Keil and Delitzsch keeps the syntax in view. The exhortation "be strong and be a man" is bound by the simple copula to the clause that follows, so that the courage David commands is courage for a single end — "to keep the commandments of the Lord." The manliness enjoined is not martial swagger but covenant fidelity; and behind it stands the promise Keil hears sounding through these verses, the word Jehovah spoke concerning David in 2 Samuel 7:12, which Solomon's obedience is now to inherit.

So the witnesses converge from their different angles. Gill and Clarke dwell on the humbling truth that a throne is no defence against the grave; Keil and Ellicott anchor the words in Joshua's example and David's covenant. All of them hear, in a dying king's counsel to a young son, the same summons: to "show thyself a man" is, at bottom, to fear God and keep his charge, since the one who must soon "go the way of all the earth" has nothing better to leave behind him.
3:
The heart of David's charge — keep the charge of the Lord thy God, to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes, and his commandments, and his judgments, and his testimonies — the witnesses treat as the noblest thing the old king says, and the standard by which the darker charges that follow must be judged. John Gill takes the "charge" broadly and particularly at once: it respects "his whole walk and conversation, and his obedience to the law and will of God; and in particular his just government of Israel committed to his charge."

Adam Clarke lingers over the five terms, making each a distinct duty — walk in his ways, keep his statutes, his commandments, his judgments, his testimonies — and then breaks into a striking meta-ethical aside. A thing, he argues, "is not good because God has commanded it; a thing is not evil because he has forbidden it. He has commanded the good, because it is in its own nature good and useful; he has forbidden the evil, because it is in its own nature bad and hurtful." God's judgments are to be kept precisely because "what he has determined to be right, is essentially and inherently right." To that thou mayest prosper he adds the plain promise: "If thou hast God's approbation, thou wilt have God's blessing."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the charge as a whole and finds it "noble enough." Solomon is to "show himself a man," in spite of his youth; to take heed in all things to follow the Law of the Lord; and to trust both the general promise of God to obedience and the special promise made to the house of David (2 Samuel 7:12–16). Ellicott hears it chiming with "the last words of David" (2 Samuel 23:3–5), that "he that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God," and with the beautiful prayer for Solomon in 1 Chronicles 29:18–19. In all this, he grants, "David speaks in the spirit of a true servant and saint of God."

But Ellicott plants here the seed of the chapter's great debate. "In the special charges that follow," he warns, "we see the worldly prudence of the old statesman, and in one case some trace of long-remembered grudge, singularly true to imperfect human nature, although utterly unworthy of an ideal picture of a hero-king." The witnesses thus set a lofty religious standard in verses 3–4 against which they will measure — and, some of them, wince at — the sentences on Joab and Shimei to come.
4:
The promise attached to obedience — that the Lord may continue his word which he spake concerning me — draws the witnesses to the conditional shape of the Davidic covenant. John Gill identifies the word plainly: it is God's "word of promise concerning the kingdom of David, and the succession of it," which faithful walking would "confirm and establish." Keil and Delitzsch trace it to its source, the great oracle of 2 Samuel 7, and note the fine grammar of the verse: the clause "that the Lord may continue his word" is subordinate to "that thou mayest prosper," so that the establishing of the dynasty flows out of Solomon's own obedient walking in God's ways. Solomon "would then experience still further this blessing of walking in the ways of the Lord, since the Lord would fulfil to him His promise of the everlasting possession of the throne."

The condition itself David quotes with care: If thy children take heed to their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart and with all their soul. The witnesses feel the weight of that if. Matthew Henry, reaching back over the charge, had drawn the crucial distinction: the promise that the Messiah should come from David's loins "was absolute; but the promise that there should not fail him a man on the throne of Israel was conditional — if his seed behave themselves as they should." Each generation, in its own day, must "keep God's charge, and then God will be sure to continue his word," for "we never let fall the promise till we let fall the precept."

The most poignant reading is Adam Clarke's. The prosperity God promised the house of David "will depend on their faithfulness to the good they receive; if they live to God, they shall sit for ever on the throne of Israel." Then, in a single mournful clause that takes in the whole tragic history the books of Kings will unfold, he adds: "But alas! they did not; and God's justice cut off the entail made by his mercy." The mercy that entailed the crown upon David's line was real; the condition was real; and the failure of the sons would, in time, be real too. There is no debate among the witnesses here, only a shared and sober theology: an unconditional grace that raises the house, and a conditional obedience on which its continuance in the throne of Israel was made to hang.
5:
With Joab the tone of the charge changes, and the witnesses divide over how to weigh it. All agree on the facts David recites: Joab "shed the blood of war in peace," murdering two captains of Israel — Abner and Amasa — and, as Adam Clarke vividly puts it, "stabbed them while he pretended to embrace them, so that their blood gushed out on his girdle, and fell into his shoes." John Gill widens the grievance to include Joab's slaying of Absalom against orders, his insolence, and his late treason in joining Adonijah, "in which he did injury to the interest, honour, and character of David."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the phrase do therefore according to thy wisdom as a call to statesmanship: quoting Sebastian Schmidt, they gloss it "mark the proper opportunity of punishing him," since "the punishment of so powerful a man as Joab the commander-in-chief... required great wisdom, to avoid occasioning a rebellion in the army, which was devoted to him." The letting of his "grey hair go down into hell in peace" means, precisely, to let him die unpunished — a thing David will not have.

The sharpest reading is Charles Ellicott's, and it opens the debate that runs through the whole chapter. He grants "a certain righteousness" in the charge: David "could not — probably since Joab's knowledge of his great crime, he dared not — punish him as he deserved," and the vivid horror of blood spirting "over the girdle and sandals of the murderer" shows how the crimes had haunted David's imagination. Ellicott parses the two murders morally. Abner's, "treacherous as it was, probably had some show of justification" in the ancient duty of the avenger of blood, which David had disclaimed without wholly condemning (2 Samuel 3:28–29, 37–39); but Amasa's "was simply one of revenge and ambition." That such crimes should be punished by a clean-handed king "was perhaps just, certainly within the letter of the law." Yet Ellicott notes what is "singularly true to nature" — that David makes no mention of the act for which he most bore Joab a grudge, "the reckless slaughter of Absalom against his own express commands." Matthew Henry answers the whole suspicion with a defence: these dying counsels "did not come from personal anger, but for the security of Solomon's throne."
6:
The clause do therefore according to thy wisdom, and let not his hoar head go down to the grave in peace presses the witnesses on a hard question: can a dying man's command that his son execute an old warrior be squared with justice? Here the commentators who elsewhere hesitate over David's motives are remarkably united that, on Joab at least, the sentence is right. John Gill reads it as a summons to prudence and timing: Solomon is to "use the wisdom he had, and take the first and fittest opportunity to cut him off for his former murders and late treason, as a dangerous man to his government and the peace of it." The wisdom lies not in whether Joab should die, but in how and when a man so entrenched in the army might safely be brought to justice.

Keil and Delitzsch, carrying over their comment from the previous verse, gloss the grim idiom exactly: to let his "grey hair go down into hell (the region of the dead) in peace" would be to let him die "un-punished," and it was precisely to prevent this that David spoke. The punishment of the commander-in-chief demanded "great wisdom, to avoid occasioning a rebellion in the army, which was devoted to him" — a note that turns the charge from private vengeance into a delicate act of state.

Most striking is the endorsement of Adam Clarke, who will later be the fiercest critic of the Shimei charge but who here finds no fault at all. "It would have been an insult to justice not to have taken the life of Joab," he writes. David himself "was culpable in delaying it so long," though "probably the circumstances of his government would not admit of his doing it sooner." The law was plain: "According to the law of God, Joab, having murdered Abner and Amasa, should die." And then the arresting conclusion — "had not David commanded Solomon to perform this act of justice, he could not have died in the approbation of his Maker." For Clarke, the charge concerning Joab is not a blot on David's dying but a duty he was bound before God to discharge. The witnesses therefore agree: whatever suspicion may attach to the later sentences, this one satisfies both the letter of the law of blood and the conscience — a murderer's grey hairs are no sanctuary from the justice he has long evaded.
7:
Between the two sentences of death stands a charge of pure gratitude, and the witnesses seize on the contrast. Show kindness unto the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite, and let them be of those that eat at thy table. Keil and Delitzsch set it in exact balance with the Joab charge: "If the demands of justice required that Joab should be punished, the duty of gratitude was no less holy to the dying king." The kindness is owed because Barzillai "had supplied David with provisions on his flight from Absalom" (2 Samuel 17:27; 2 Samuel 19:32). They press the precise sense of the Hebrew: "let them be among those eating of thy table" means not merely to draw rations from the royal kitchen, as all the court did, but "let them join in the meals at the royal table" — a place of honour and intimacy.

John Gill supposes Barzillai himself "was now dead, and therefore he would have kindness shown to his posterity for his sake," while Charles Ellicott notes how the charge "stands out in pleasant contrast" to those around it, and follows the thread of gratitude forward: the mention of "the habitation of Chimham" near Bethlehem (Jeremiah 41:17) suggests David had already given Barzillai's son an inheritance "out of what was especially his own," his native ground.

It is Matthew Henry who draws the pastoral lesson most warmly. When David on his deathbed "was remembering the injuries that had been done, he could not forget the kindnesses that had been shown, but leaves it as a charge upon his son to return them." From this Henry frames a rule of grateful memory: "the kindnesses we have received from our friends must not be buried either in their graves or ours, but our children must return them to theirs." He even suggests that Solomon later distilled this very charge into a proverb — "Thy own friend, and thy father's friend, forsake not" (Proverbs 27:10) — and compares Paul's prayer for the household of Onesiphorus, who "had often refreshed him." So the witnesses find in this small clause the balance of David's whole charge: the same king who will not let a murderer's guilt sleep will not let a benefactor's kindness be forgotten, and both justice and gratitude are made a sacred trust to pass to the next generation.
8:
With Shimei the chapter reaches its most contested charge, and the witnesses openly disagree over its justice. The facts are not in dispute: Shimei, "a Benjamite of Bahurim," had "cursed me with a grievous curse in the day when I went to Mahanaim," and David had afterward sworn to spare his life. The division is over what David is now doing with that oath. John Gill reads the man simply as a hidden enemy — now with Solomon and "on his side, and of his party," but "not to be trusted, or looked upon as a real friend."

Keil and Delitzsch give the charge a theological justification. Shimei had cursed David "with a vehement curse as he fled from Absalom" (2 Samuel 16:5–8); David, unwilling to "mar the joy at his reinstatement" by an execution, had personally forgiven him (2 Samuel 19:19–24). But, Keil insists, "the insult which Shimei had offered in his person to the anointed of the Lord, as king and representative of the rights of God, he could not forgive." The private wrong David remitted; the public affront to the Lord's anointed remained a debt to be reckoned.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the opposite view and states the debate at its sharpest. This is "the most ungenerous charge," being "the virtual withdrawal of the pardon, freely granted to Shimei long before" (2 Samuel 19:18–23). He allows that it "is, perhaps, partly dictated by policy," for Shimei was "powerful" and had "assumed a dangerous championship of the fallen house of Saul." Yet he cannot excuse it: "there are unmistakable traces of the old grudge rankling in David's heart, reminding us of the bitterness of such psalms as" the sixty-ninth (Psalm 69). So the witnesses stand divided over the same words. To Keil the charge defends the sacredness of the anointed office; to Ellicott it betrays a wound that even a saint could not forgive; and Gill, keeping to the plain sense, only warns that a smiling enemy is an enemy still. All feel the difficulty Adam Clarke will press to breaking point — that David seems to command against the very oath by which he had bound himself.
9:
The command now therefore hold him not guiltless... but his hoar head bring thou down to the grave with blood forces the witnesses to reconcile David's oath of pardon with his dying instruction, and here Adam Clarke introduces the boldest reading in the whole chapter. John Gill keeps to the surface: David bids Solomon "not look upon him as an innocent person; and if he commits an offence against thee, as he has against me, do not acquit him as I have done." The pardon was David's to give and need not bind his heir if Shimei should offend again.

Keil and Delitzsch read the words as a genuine, if guarded, sentence of justice: Solomon is "a wise man" who "knowest what thou oughtest to do unto him." But Clarke cannot let the plain sense stand, for it would make a dying prophet order the death of a man he had sworn to spare. He proposes a grammatical rescue drawn from Kennicott: "the negative particle lo... in the former clause, hold him not guiltless, should be repeated in this latter clause, though not expressed" — so that the true meaning is "his hoary head bring thou not down to the grave with blood." Clarke insists such carrying-over of an unexpressed negative "frequently" occurs in Hebrew, and that on this reading David commands not vengeance but continued restraint: treat him as the dangerous man he is, "only as I have sworn to him, and he is an aged man, let him not die a violent death."

The disagreement is real and unresolved among the witnesses. To Gill and Keil the verse licenses Solomon to punish a proven offender without breaking any oath, since David swore only for his own lifetime; to Clarke the verse, rightly pointed, actually forbids the shedding of Shimei's blood, and it is only Solomon's own later reckoning that brings the old man down. Clarke offers a telling proof from the sequel: Solomon did not in fact kill Shimei on this charge, but confined him to Jerusalem and spared him for years — behaviour that fits a command of restraint better than a command of death. The witnesses leave the reader with two Davids: one who guards his son's throne by handing on an unfinished justice, and one who, even on his deathbed, holds fast the oath he swore by the Lord.
10:
So David slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David. The witnesses read the gentle phrase for death with care and gather round David's tomb. John Gill notes the delicacy of "slept with his fathers": since David was not laid in his ancestors' burying-place, the words cannot mean he lay with them in the grave, but rather that he "died as his ancestors before him did" and joined them "in the state of the dead." He adds the chronologers' reckoning, that David died about the feast of Pentecost, in the year of the world 2990, some 1014 years before Christ.

Matthew Henry lifts the notice into worship. "Now David, after he had served his own generation, by the will of God, fell asleep, and was laid to his fathers, and saw corruption" — the very words Paul preached at Antioch (Acts 13:36). His epitaph, Henry says, may be taken from "the last words of David" (2 Samuel 23:1): here lies "the man who was raised up on high, the anointed of the God of Jacob, and the sweet psalmist of Israel"; and to it David adds his own hope, "My flesh also shall rest in hope" (Psalm 16:9).

The grammatical-historical witnesses attend to the place. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott fix the burial "in Mount Zion," where the sepulchres of David became "the regular tombs of the kings" (Nehemiah 3:16; Ezekiel 43:7–9); Ellicott adds that it was reckoned a signal honour that the high priest Jehoiada was later buried there (2 Chronicles 24:16). Both Keil and Adam Clarke observe that the tomb still stood in the apostles' day, for Peter could say that David "is both dead and buried, and his sepulchre is with us unto this day" (Acts 2:29). Clarke and Gill relay the tradition from Josephus that vast treasures were buried with the king, later rifled by Hyrcanus and Herod, though Clarke judges "little credit is due to this account." Beneath the antiquarian detail the witnesses land together on Henry's note: the mightiest king of Israel, having "served his own generation by the will of God," saw corruption like other men — while the Son of David whom Peter proclaims from this same grave did not, and it is to Him the whole scene silently points.
11:
The reign of David is sealed with a round number before the throne passes to his son: the days that David reigned over Israel were forty years: seven years reigned he in Hebron, and thirty and three years reigned he in Jerusalem. John Gill confirms the figure from outside Scripture, citing the pagan historian Eupolemus that David reigned forty years, and, as elsewhere, notes that the odd months of his Hebron reign are dropped so that only the even sum is reckoned.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat verses 11–12 as a hinge in the narrative — a heading "more fully expanded in 1 Chronicles 29:23–25" — and lay out the programme of the chapter's second half. Solomon "established his monarchy first of all by punishing the rebels, Adonijah and his adherents, and by carrying out the final instructions of his father." Everything that follows — the deaths of Adonijah, Joab, and Shimei, and the deposing of Abiathar — is thus gathered under the single announcement that "his kingdom was established greatly."

Keil sees the seam already opening onto the next scene: Adonijah the son of Haggith came to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon. The forty-year reign is not merely a chronicler's full stop; it closes the life whose covenant the whole chapter has been transmitting, and opens the account of how the son must secure by justice what the father had won by grace. The witnesses, sparing of comment on the bare arithmetic, let the number carry its own weight: a long reign that began in a cave at Adullam and a crowning at Hebron ends with a throne so settled that a young king can move at once against every threat to it. What David charged in his weakness, Solomon will now execute in his strength, and the historian's tidy sum of years marks the passing of one age of the covenant into another. Behind the reckoning stands the promise that had made David king at all, and that Keil hears fulfilled here — that the Lord who "hath established me and set me on the throne of my father David" will keep the house he has built.
12:
Then sat Solomon upon the throne of David his father; and his kingdom was established greatly. The witnesses mark first how smoothly the succession runs. John Gill observes that Solomon had already sat on the throne "in his lifetime, with his consent, and by his order," and now held it "by the agreement of the whole people." Matthew Henry savours the contrast with David's own hard road to the crown: Solomon "came to it much more easily and peaceably than David did, and much sooner saw his government established." He draws the happy maxim, "It is happy for a kingdom when the end of one good reign is the beginning of another."

Yet Henry and the others agree that the phrase established greatly anticipates a work not yet finished, for the throne had still to be cleared of its rivals. Henry frames the sequel as "the just and necessary removal of Adonijah his rival, in order to the establishment of his throne." Adonijah, having failed once, "might have been safe" had he stayed quiet; instead he betrays himself, and "the righteous God" leaves him "to himself, that he might be punished for his former treason and that Solomon's throne might be established." From this Henry preaches a warning: "Many thus ruin themselves, because they know not when they are well off... and sinners, by presuming on God's patience, treasure up wrath to themselves."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same words backward from the chapter's close. That the summary is repeated in the final verse — that after the deaths of Adonijah, Joab, and Shimei "the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon" — suggests to him that "under the smooth surface of apparent loyalty, there lurked some elements of disaffection and danger," perhaps sharpened by enemies abroad, for the death of David was the signal for attempts at rebellion in the conquered nations (1 Kings 11:14–25). These, Ellicott notes, "are apparently crushed without the slightest effort, though with no little fierceness and severity; and the royalty of Solomon rises at once to a colossal greatness." So the witnesses hold the verse in tension: a peaceable accession that must yet be secured by a season of blood, and a greatness that is real precisely because the young king does not flinch from the dangers hidden beneath the calm.
13:
The scene shifts to a private interview whose danger only one party understands. Adonijah the son of Haggith came to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon. And she said, Comest thou peaceably? And he said, Peaceably. John Gill, following Abarbinel, sets the visit "a few days after the death of David," when the new reign was barely settled. Keil and Delitzsch catch the wariness in Bathsheba's greeting. Her question, "Is peace thy coming?" — "comest thou with a peaceable intention?" — is asked "because after what had occurred she suspected an evil intention," remembering Adonijah's earlier bid for the crown (1 Kings 1:5). The very defeated pretender who had grasped at the kingdom now stands, disarmingly, in the queen-mother's chamber.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns the encounter into a lesson in Eastern court custom. Adonijah's decision to approach Bathsheba rather than Solomon directly, and the marked honour the king will presently pay her, "illustrate the universal custom of Eastern monarchies," in which "the wives of the king, being many, are seldom held to be of any great political account," but "the mother of the reigning king is a person of great dignity and influence." Ellicott notes how the historian confirms this by recording "the name of each king's mother" throughout the book. Bathsheba is therefore exactly the right advocate — a woman of real weight at court, and, crucially, a mother inclined to think the best of a suppliant.

The witnesses do not yet dispute Adonijah's motive, but they lay the ground for the coming debate. To Keil the wary greeting shows that Bathsheba, at least, knows a defeated rival may not come in good faith; to Ellicott the whole approach is a calculated use of the one channel most likely to disarm suspicion. The dramatic irony hangs over the reader: Adonijah answers "Peaceably," and Bathsheba, unlike Solomon after her, will take the word at face value. The stage is set for a request that looks like a small favour of the heart and will prove, in the king's eyes, a fresh reach after the throne — the difference between what a trusting mother hears and what a watchful king sees being the very hinge on which Adonijah's life will turn.
14:
The interview advances by courtly indirection. He said, moreover, I have something to say unto thee. And she said, Say on. John Gill reads the preface simply: Adonijah is "signifying that he came upon business," not on a mere social call. The formality is itself a signal — a man who has just protested that he comes "peaceably" now asks leave to broach a matter, feeling his way toward a request he dare not make bluntly.

Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment gathers up the run of dialogue from here through Bathsheba's arrival before the king, let the verse do its quiet work of framing. Adonijah first secures her attention and her goodwill — "Say on" — before he unfolds either the grievance ("Thou knowest that the kingdom was mine") or the petition ("that he give me Abishag the Shunammite to wife") that follow. The structure is that of a practised suitor: win the hearer, rehearse the loss, then name the small thing asked. Keil notes that when Bathsheba in turn carries the matter to Solomon, "he received her with the reverence due to the queen-mother," rising to meet her — so that the chain of courtesies Adonijah sets in motion runs all the way to the throne room.

The witnesses find in this brief exchange the calculated patience of the request. Adonijah does not blurt out his desire; he asks for a hearing, and only then for a favour. Bathsheba, unsuspecting, grants the first as readily as she will grant the second. The reader, forewarned by the wary greeting of the previous verse, watches the trap being baited with the smoothest of manners. What passes between them as the courtesy of a chastened prince and a gracious queen-mother will be read by Solomon as the opening move of a renewed conspiracy — and the very indirection that makes the approach seem harmless to Bathsheba is what will make it seem, to her royal son, so evidently designed. The scene turns entirely on this gap between the innocence of the listener and the intent the king will impute to the speaker.
15:
Adonijah opens his suit with a careful recital of loss: Thou knowest that the kingdom was mine, and all Israel set their faces on me, that I should reign: howbeit the kingdom is turned about, and is become my brother's: for it was his from the Lord. The witnesses weigh both the claim and the craft behind it. John Gill grants the kernel of right — the crown "belonged to him by virtue of his birthright; he was heir to it, being the eldest son." Adam Clarke enlarges the claim into a threefold title and then trumps it. The kingdom "certainly was his by the right of primogeniture, and it was his by the voice of the people, and the consent of the high priest. But there was a right paramount to all these, the right of God; it was his kingdom... and he had a right to give the crown to whomsoever he pleased, and he was pleased to give it to Solomon."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the speech as pure manoeuvre. Adonijah's words "show craft, flattering Bath-sheba by extolling her influence, and making merit of his surrender to Solomon, in obedience to the Lord's decree, of a kingdom which, in evident contradiction to fact, he asserts to have been destined to him by popular desire." Keil and Delitzsch temper this: the throne was in a sense really his "as the eldest son at that time, according to the right of primogeniture," and he had indeed found adherents in Joab and Abiathar — yet his pious "it was his from the Lord" sits oddly with the rebellion he had lately raised.

Ellicott alone lifts the veil on the request the recital is meant to introduce. The petition for Abishag, "apparently harmless, and (since Abishag was concubine of David only in name) involving nothing unnatural, had perhaps a covert design: for, by universal Eastern custom, to take a king's wives was the known privilege or duty of his successor." He recalls the counsel of Ahithophel to Absalom (2 Samuel 16:21) as proof of the principle. "If, therefore, Adonijah had publicly espoused Abishag, it might have seemed a virtual renewal of his claim to the crown. This Solomon sees at once, though Bath-sheba, strangely enough, does not see it." So the witnesses expose the double bottom of Adonijah's humble speech: a true title genuinely surrendered, and a suit for a woman that may be a mask for the throne he claims to have let go.
16:
And now I ask one petition of thee, deny me not. Having rehearsed his loss, Adonijah arrives at the request, and the witnesses fasten on the rhetoric of the single small favour. John Gill notes the deliberate diminishment: it is "one petition... and but one, and a small one too, as Bathsheba herself after calls it." The whole art of the appeal lies in that word one. A man who has surrendered a kingdom asks now for a single thing — how could so trifling a boon be refused?

Keil and Delitzsch, whose exposition runs across these verses, show that the smallness is precisely the snare. What Bathsheba "regarded in her womanly simplicity as a very small one" Solomon will detect at once as an intrigue reaching for the throne. The petition is framed to slip past a mother's guard: it comes wrapped in humility ("deny me not"), reduced to a single item, and addressed to the one advocate least likely to suspect it. Adonijah has already conceded that the kingdom is Solomon's "from the Lord"; his very piety in the previous breath makes the modest request seem the more innocent.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the hinge of the whole scene. Its plain words are a suppliant's courtesy — the language of a beaten rival content with a crumb from the table he once hoped to own. But under Gill's and Keil's reading the courtesy is calculated: to ask "one petition" and to press "deny me not" is to bind the hearer by her own kindness before the substance is named. Bathsheba's readiness to grant a "small" thing, and Solomon's refusal to see it as small at all, will be the measure of the distance between them. The verse thus prepares the reversal to come: the petition Adonijah presents as the least of favours the king will treat as a capital offence, and the little word "one" — meant to make the request irresistible — will instead expose how much a defeated pretender still dares to want. What looks like the humblest of askings is, on the witnesses' reading, the boldest of moves dressed in the meekest of words.
17:
The petition is finally named — that he give me Abishag the Shunammite to wife — and over it the witnesses divide most sharply in the whole chapter. John Gill notes that Adonijah frames it flatteringly, owning Solomon as "the king" precisely "to engage her to take his suit in hand, and to cover his design." Adam Clarke, by contrast, takes the request at face value and makes it the ground of a fierce indictment of Solomon. Adonijah "cheerfully gives up all right to the kingdom, and only desires to have this young woman," who, though David's in name, "was still in a state of virginity." Clarke reports the common suspicion — that Joab and Abiathar had prompted the suit, hoping "the popular tide would again turn in his favour" — but rejects it: "there is little evidence to support these conjectures... It does not appear that Adonijah by desiring to have Abishag had any thought of the kingdom... though Solomon appears to have understood him in this sense."

From this Clarke mounts his protest against reading the request as treason. Without further evidence, he says, it was "a flimsy pretext to imbrue his hands in a brother's blood." He reaches for the fable of the wolf and the lamb and the old proverb — "It is an easy thing to find a staff to beat a dog with... We readily find an excuse for whatever we are determined to do" — and then delivers his hardest blow: "He who attempts to varnish over this conduct of Solomon by either state necessity or a Divine command, is an enemy, in my mind, to the cause of God and truth."

Against Clarke stand the other witnesses, who read the suit exactly as Solomon did. For Gill it is a "design" to be covered; for Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott the Eastern custom that made a king's wife the successor's prize gives the request a plain political edge. Here the debate is stated at full pitch: was Adonijah an innocent man asking for a wife and murdered on a pretext, or a persistent conspirator making one more veiled bid for the crown? Clarke will not excuse the execution on any plea of policy or providence; the grammatical-historical company and the Reformed Gill judge that Solomon rightly discerned, beneath a modest suit, the same ambition that had already once armed itself against the throne.
18:
And Bathsheba said, Well; I will speak for thee unto the king. The queen-mother consents, and the witnesses read her ready assent as the very picture of unsuspecting goodwill. John Gill catches her whole meaning in a phrase: "Very well spoken, the thing is good and right." Bathsheba hears no plot; she hears a modest request from a chastened prince, and undertakes it as a kindness.

This is where Matthew Henry, surveying the scene, marvels at her blindness. "It was strange that she did not suspect the treason, but more strange that she did not abhor the incest, that was in the proposal." Henry supposes she may not have reckoned Abishag truly David's wife, "because the marriage was not consummated," or thought the point "might be dispensed with to gratify Adonijah, in consideration of his tame submission." Either way, he judges plainly, "this was her weakness and folly: it was well that she was not regent." From it he distils a rule for all who have influence with the great: those who "have the ear of princes... as it is their wisdom not to be too prodigal of their interest, so it is their duty never to use it for the assistance of sin or the furtherance of any wicked design. Let not princes be asked that which they ought not to grant."

The witnesses thus turn Bathsheba's little word "Well" into a study of misplaced kindness. Her fault is not malice but credulity — a mother willing to believe the best, and to lend her weight to a suit she has not examined. Gill's approving gloss shows how the request appeared to her; Henry's severer verdict shows how it appeared to God and to the watchful king. The lesson the company draws is pastoral rather than polemical: good will is not enough where discernment is wanting, and to carry another's petition without weighing its justice is to make oneself the instrument of a design one never intended. Bathsheba means only to be gracious; she becomes, unawares, the messenger of Adonijah's ruin — proof, the witnesses would say, that even the kindest advocacy must be governed by a care for what is right.
19:
Bathsheba's approach to the throne becomes, for the witnesses, a tableau of filial honour. The king rose up to meet her, and bowed himself unto her, and sat down on his throne, and caused a seat to be set for the king's mother; and she sat on his right hand. John Gill pictures her coming from her own apartment with her suit, while Keil and Delitzsch underline the ceremony: Solomon "received her with the reverence due to the queen-mother," rising from his throne to greet her.

Matthew Henry draws the moral out at length. Solomon "received her with all the respect that was due to a mother, though he himself was a king: he rose up to meet her, bowed himself to her, and caused her to sit on his right hand, according to the law of the fifth commandment." From this he presses a duty that greatness does not cancel: "Children, not only when grown up, but when grown great, must give honour to their parents, and behave dutifully and respectfully towards them. Despise not thy mother when she is old." The seat at the right hand, the place of honour, shows a king who will not let his crown excuse him from reverence to the woman who bore him.

Yet the witnesses notice, with Charles Ellicott, that the honour is also of a piece with the Eastern dignity of the queen-mother — a rank of "great dignity and influence" quite distinct from that of a king's many wives. The very deference that expresses Solomon's piety also raises the stakes of the moment: because he has promised his mother a hearing and seated her in the place of honour, her petition comes clothed in every advantage, and his refusal, when it comes, will be the more striking. Henry adds that Solomon's promise "not to say her nay" was understood by both "with this necessary limitation, provided it be just and reasonable and fit to be granted." So the scene holds two truths together: a son's exemplary honouring of his mother, and a king's clear resolve that not even a mother, honoured at his right hand, may draw him into granting what is wrong. The bow and the throne, the reverence and the coming refusal, belong to the same wisdom.
20:
The petition is presented under the guise of a trifle. I desire one small petition of thee; I pray thee, say me not nay. John Gill hears the same diminishing note Adonijah had struck: "it was but one, and a little one, and therefore she hoped it would be granted." Keil and Delitzsch render the exchange with its full emotional colour. To the request "which she regarded in her womanly simplicity as a very small one," Solomon answers first with warmth — "Ask on, my mother: for I will not say thee nay" — and then, the moment Abishag's name is spoken, "with indignation, detecting at once the intrigues of Adonijah."

Keil quotes the king's stinging reply as it breaks out in the next verse: "And why dost thou ask Abishag of Shunem for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom, for he is my elder brother; and indeed for him, and for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab the son of Zeruiah." The repeated "for him," they note, "answers entirely to the emotional character of the words." Solomon names the two great men — the priest and the general — "because these two men of high rank had supported Adonijah's rebellion and wished to rule under his name." What Bathsheba brought as a small kindness, the king reads as the visible tip of a standing conspiracy.

The witnesses find in this verse the pivot on which the scene turns from courtesy to condemnation. Solomon's opening promise, made to honour his mother, is genuine; but it is bounded, as Henry had said, by justice, and the instant the request proves unjust the promise cannot hold. The gap between "one small petition" and "ask for him the kingdom also" measures everything: the same words that seem to Bathsheba the least of favours reveal to Solomon a reach after the throne itself. Keil defends the king's insight against the critics who would soften it — "there is no ground for any such alterations of the text as Thenius proposes" — insisting the vehemence is authentic and the reading sound. So the little petition, meant to be irresistible, becomes the occasion of Adonijah's death, and the mother's innocent errand the means by which the last conspirator unmasks himself before the throne.
21:
Bathsheba names the request in the plainest terms, and even leans on kinship to commend it: Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah thy brother to wife. John Gill catches the touch of a mother's advocacy — she "makes use of the relation, the more to move upon him to grant the request," reminding the king that the petitioner is his own brother by their father's side. What she urges as a bond of family Solomon will hear as the badge of a rival.

Keil and Delitzsch let the verse run straight into the king's flashing answer. The moment the name is spoken, Solomon retorts, "And why dost thou ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom also; for he is mine elder brother; even for him, and for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab the son of Zeruiah." The word brother, which Bathsheba offered as a reason for mercy, becomes in Solomon's mouth the reason for alarm — an elder brother, backed by a priest and a general, asking for the late king's wife is asking, in effect, for the crown. Keil notes that the very phrasing, with its repeated "for him," carries the heat of the king's discernment.

The witnesses read the exchange as the collision of two innocent-seeming words with a deadly logic. Bathsheba's appeal to brotherhood and to the smallness of the boon meets Solomon's instant reckoning of what such a marriage would signify by the custom of the East. Keil adds that Solomon at once solemnly swears — "God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah have not spoken this word against his own life" — the oath by which the suit turns into a sentence. To ask for Abishag, on the king's reading, was to speak "against his own life," at the cost of his own head. So the verse marks the exact point where the mother's kindness and the brother's craft are overtaken by the king's judgment: what was framed as a family favour is unmasked as a capital design, and the plea of kinship, far from shielding Adonijah, only sharpens the danger, for it is precisely as the elder brother that he is most to be feared.
22:
Solomon's reply flashes with a heat the witnesses cannot miss. And why dost thou ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom also; for he is mine elder brother. John Gill reads the king as answering "with as much gentleness and mildness as he could, but inwardly fired at her request, and amazed at it," so that "he could not forbear using some degree of tartness and resentment." Beneath the courtesy owed a mother burns real anger at the design he has detected.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the same fire and reads its meaning. "In Solomon's answer there is a certain bitterness, venting itself in irony, which seems to argue the mingling with kingly dignity and policy of some passionate feeling, not unlike the bursts of passion in his father, as in the case of Nabal" (1 Samuel 25:21–22). Ellicott ventures a further conjecture: this heat "gives some probability to the conjecture that Abishag was the 'fair Shulamite' of the Song of Solomon, already loved by the youthful king" (Song of Solomon 6:13) — so that a personal as well as a political motive may quicken his indignation. Either way, "in his wrath he infers, rightly or wrongly, that the hand of the conspirators is seen in this petition, and executes vengeance accordingly, summarily and without giving them any trial or opportunity of excusing themselves."

The witnesses thus present a king in whom judgment and passion are hard to separate. Solomon reasons soundly — an elder brother's suit for the late king's wife is a reach for the throne — and Ellicott grants the inference may be right; yet the manner is peremptory, the sentence swift, and no defence is heard. Gill's "tartness" and Ellicott's "bitterness" agree that the moment is charged with feeling as well as statecraft. The verse leaves standing the chapter's great question in its sharpest personal form: is this the clear-eyed vigilance of a king securing the throne God gave him, or the passionate stroke of a young ruler who reads treason into a rival's request and strikes before the case is proved? The witnesses record both the shrewdness and the vehemence, and do not pretend the two can be neatly told apart.
23:
Solomon binds his verdict with an oath: God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah have not spoken this word against his own life. John Gill reads the swearing as a way of closing the matter against further pleading — Solomon speaks "to prevent his mother pressing him to have her petition granted." The oath makes the sentence irrevocable and shuts the door on appeal.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the ancient formula itself. "God do so to me, and more also" is a "well-known formula of imprecation," and he lines up its recurrences across the histories — from Ruth's vow (Ruth 1:17) to Eli's charge to Samuel (1 Samuel 3:17) and Saul's rash oaths (1 Samuel 14:44). The Septuagint renders it, "May God do these things to me and add these things also," and Ellicott suggests the words "probably" were "accompanied with some gesture signifying utter destruction" — a slashing motion invoking the same doom on the swearer should he fail to keep his word.

Keil and Delitzsch take the substance of the oath in earnest: "Adonijah has spoken this word against his own life," at the hazard of his life and to his own destruction. The petition was, in law, self-condemning; by asking for Abishag the pretender had pronounced his own sentence. The witnesses read the verse, then, as the point where Solomon's private discernment becomes a public and binding act. The oath is not mere vehemence but the solemn form by which a king commits himself before God to a course of justice — the same form his father David had used, and the patriarchs and judges before him. Where the previous verses showed the king's anger, this one shows his resolve hardening into a vow that no maternal pressure can bend. Adonijah's fate is now sealed not by a passing burst of feeling but by an appeal to God as witness and avenger, and the witnesses hear in it both the gravity of the occasion and the settled purpose of a throne determined to suffer no second rising.
24:
The oath rises to its height: Now therefore, as the Lord liveth, which hath established me, and set me on the throne of David my father, and who hath made me an house, as he promised, Adonijah shall be put to death this day. John Gill feels the temperature of the words — this "is another oath," and "one may easily perceive hereby in what a temper and disposition Solomon was, how warm, earnest, and vehement, how resolute against the petition."

Keil and Delitzsch defend the justice of the sentence that follows. The king "was perfectly just in doing this," for Adonijah, "even after his first attempt to seize upon the throne had been forgiven by Solomon, endeavoured to secure his end by fresh machinations." On this reading the execution is no fit of temper but a duty owed to God: "duty to God, who had exalted Solomon to the throne, demanded that the rebel should be punished with all the severity of the law, without regard to blood-relationship." Solomon grounds his oath in the very promise his father had charged him to trust — the word that "hath made me an house," quoted almost verbatim from 2 Samuel 7:11 — so that the God who established the throne is invoked as the guarantor of its cleansing.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the adjuration as a window into Solomon's very character, setting it beside David's oath in the previous chapter (1 Kings 1:29). "In David we always see the living man, whose soul longs after God with a vivid personal devotion. Solomon is emphatically the king, sitting on the throne of David, with his house established for ever. In the majesty of his royalty his individual character is to us almost entirely merged." The witnesses thus hold together the warmth Gill notes and the majesty Ellicott describes: a young king, vehement in the moment, yet swearing by the covenant-keeping God and grounding his act in the promise of an established house. Whether one weighs the heat or the right of it, all agree the sentence is now fixed — "this day" Adonijah shall die — and that Solomon reads the securing of his throne as inseparable from the faithfulness of the God who gave it.
25:
And king Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; and he fell upon him that he died. The swift execution divides the witnesses more sharply than any single act in the chapter. John Gill notes only the agents — Benaiah, perhaps with some of the Cherethites and Pelethites under him. Charles Ellicott observes the office: "the chief of the body-guard is the chief of 'the executioners'" (1 Kings 1:38), carrying out the sentence "with his own hand," as in the old days of the judges (Judges 8:20–21).

Adam Clarke turns the verse into a protest. He is scandalised that so great a general should serve as headsman — Benaiah "seems to have been the public state executioner, and yet he was generalissimo of all the forces" — and, most of all, at the manner of Adonijah's death: "He is suspected, condemned, and slain, without a hearing." Clarke then confronts the classic defence head on. He quotes Calmet's maxim that "a prince, in his judgments, cannot always follow the rules of the most perfect morality; policy and the good of the state often require things to be done which are contrary to the counsels of the Gospel," and denounces it — "What a diabolic maxim is this!" — closing with a burst of English gratitude for a land governed by law rather than by such reasoning.

Keil and Delitzsch answer from the opposite side: the king "was perfectly just," for Adonijah's fresh machinations after a first pardon left duty to God no choice but the full severity of the law. So the two readings stand unreconciled over the same corpse. To Clarke the execution is judicial murder, a brother slain on suspicion without trial, and no plea of state necessity can cleanse it; to Keil it is righteous justice on a persistent rebel, demanded by the God who set Solomon on the throne. The witnesses agree only on the facts — a word from the king, a blow from Benaiah, and the elder brother dead the same day — and leave the reader to weigh the ancient question they will not settle: whether the security of a God-given throne may be bought with a summary sentence, or whether even a king's clear suspicion must yield to the forms of a fair hearing.
26:
With Abiathar the chapter shows mercy tempering justice. Get thee to Anathoth, unto thine own fields; for thou art worthy of death: but I will not at this time put thee to death, because thou barest the ark of the Lord God before David my father. Keil and Delitzsch read Solomon's restraint as proof "how free his actions were from personal revenge or too great severity." Abiathar "had also forfeited his life through the part he took in Adonijah's conspiracy," yet the king merely banishes him to his estate, telling him plainly, "Thou art indeed a man of death... but I will not put thee to death to-day," and giving the reason: he had borne the ark of Jehovah — both in its solemn conveyance to Jerusalem (1 Chronicles 15:11) and in David's flight from Absalom (2 Samuel 15:24) — and had shared all David's afflictions.

Matthew Henry spells out the leniency and its ground. In consideration of his old services Abiathar "is only degraded," deposed from office and confined to Anathoth, "forbidden the court, the city, the tabernacle, the altar, and all inter-meddling in public business," yet spared. Henry sets the mercy in sharp relief: "Saul, for a supposed crime, had barbarously slain Abiathar's father, and eighty-five priests, their families, and city. Solomon spares Abiathar himself, though guilty of a real crime. Thus was Saul's government ruined and Solomon's established." And he draws the pastoral rule: "Those that show kindness to God's people shall have it remembered to their advantage one time or other."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott locates Anathoth precisely — a priestly city in Benjamin a few miles from Jerusalem, best known as the birthplace of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:1) — and sharpens the point of the pardon: "it is not Abiathar's priestly character which protects him, but the remembrance of his long friendship to David in adversity," and perhaps a special promise David had made him after Saul's slaughter of his house (1 Samuel 22:20–23). The witnesses agree that Abiathar deserved death and was spared for gratitude's sake; the same king who struck Adonijah without a hearing here stays his hand for a man who had carried the ark through David's darkest days.
27:
The narrator pauses to name a prophecy fulfilled. So Solomon thrust out Abiathar from being priest unto the Lord; that he might fulfil the word of the Lord, which he spake concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh. John Gill stresses that the deposition was Solomon's own royal act — "done by his own authority as a king, and not as a prophet" — dismissing the notion, urged by some Roman controversialists, that it required the authority of a college of priests.

Keil and Delitzsch and Adam Clarke read the verse as the closing of a long account with the house of Eli. The banishment "involved his deposition from the priesthood," and thereby, Keil notes, "was the word of the Lord concerning the house of Eli fulfilled" (1 Samuel 2:30–33); he glosses the Hebrew "that he might fulfil" as answering to the New Testament "that it might be fulfilled." Clarke traces the reversal in the priestly lines: "God had told Eli that the priesthood should depart from his house; Abiathar was the last of the descendants of Ithamar... Zadok, who was made priest in the stead of Abiathar, was of the family of Eleazar; and by this change the priesthood reverted to its ancient channel." Abiathar "deserved this degradation" for supporting Adonijah "even during the life of his father."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott refines the theology of the fulfilment. The words describe "not the purpose, but the effect of Solomon's action" — the human deed of a young king, discharging his own justice, is at the same time the vehicle of a divine sentence spoken generations before (1 Samuel 2:30–35; 1 Samuel 3:11–14). By it "the house of Ithamar" is degraded in Abiathar's person and "the house of Eleazar" restored in Zadok, "to whom, as the elder son of Aaron, the primacy would have seemed naturally to belong." The witnesses converge on a single, weighty point: what looks like mere court politics — one priest disgraced, another advanced — is read by the sacred historian as the long arm of God's word overtaking the house of Eli at last, so that a threat uttered at Shiloh in Samuel's boyhood finds its quiet completion in Solomon's decree. Human justice and divine prophecy, they agree, run here in the very same channel.
28:
The news drives Joab to sanctuary. Then tidings came to Joab... and Joab fled unto the tabernacle of the Lord, and caught hold on the horns of the altar. John Gill supplies the trigger — word of "the death of Adonijah, and the deposition of Abiathar." Keil and Delitzsch fix the place as "the holy tent upon Zion," where Joab fled "to seek protection at the altar," and explain the parenthesis that Joab "had leaned after Adonijah... but not after Absalom," dismissing the Septuagint's variant reading (that he had turned after Solomon) as an arbitrary alteration.

Adam Clarke weighs the old warrior's motive with unusual tenderness. Fearing for his safety after Adonijah's death and Abiathar's banishment, Joab "takes refuge at the tabernacle, as claiming Divine protection, and desiring to have his case decided by God alone"; or perhaps, Clarke allows, "a spark of remorse is now kindled; and, knowing that he must die, he wishes to die in the house of God."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the scene politically and legally. He finds it "strange that Joab should have been in no danger or anxiety immediately after the actual failure of the conspiracy," and notes that although the real motive for his death was his renewed support of Adonijah, "Solomon's words in pronouncing sentence on him refrain from mention of anything except the old crimes." Ellicott suggests the reason: to bring Joab's case within the emphatic declaration of the Law "that no sanctuary should protect the wilful and treacherous murderer, and that innocent blood, so shed and left unavenged, would pollute the land" (Exodus 21:14; Numbers 35:33). He marks, too, the increased power of the young monarchy: the old captain of the host, who had once been "too hard" for David himself, now falls "without a single act of resistance," and — a grim providence — near the very "great stone in Gibeon" where he had murdered Amasa (2 Samuel 20:8). The witnesses hold together the man's dread, his possible remorse, and the law that will reach him even at the horns of the altar.
29:
And it was told king Solomon that Joab was fled unto the tabernacle of the Lord; and, behold, he is by the altar. Then Solomon sent Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, saying, Go, fall upon him. John Gill pictures the report reaching the king "by some of his courtiers," setting in motion the same executioner who had struck down Adonijah.

Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment gathers up the exchange that follows, show the king's first order and its ground. When Benaiah hesitates and Joab refuses to leave the altar — "Nay; but I will die here" — Solomon answers, "Do as he hath said, and fall upon him, and bury him; that thou mayest take away the innocent blood, which Joab shed, from me, and from the house of my father." The command is deliberately linked to the demands of blood-guilt: Joab is to die precisely so that "the innocent blood" of Abner and Amasa may be lifted from David's house. Keil reads the whole sequence as the execution of a just sentence long deferred, now carried out with due form even upon a man clinging to the horns of the altar.

The witnesses find in this brief verse the collision of two sanctities — the holiness of the altar and the sanctity of innocent blood — and they know which the Law makes yield. Joab has reckoned that the sacred tent will shelter him, as it had sheltered many a lesser offender; Solomon, informed and unhesitating, sends Benaiah with orders that treat the altar as no refuge for a wilful murderer. The reader hears, in the plain report and the curt command, the same principle the previous verse announced from Moses: that the place of atonement cannot be made a hiding-place for guilt that atonement was never meant to cover. What looks at first like a violation of holy ground is, on the witnesses' reading, the vindication of it — for an altar that shielded the shedder of innocent blood would itself be profaned. Solomon's message, "Go, fall upon him," thus carries forward both his father's dying charge and the settled justice of the God whose altar Joab has grasped in vain.
30:
Benaiah's errand meets a strange defiance. And Benaiah came to the tabernacle of the Lord, and said unto him, Thus saith the king, Come forth. And he said, Nay; but I will die here. John Gill locates the scene at Gibeon, where the great high place stood, and Joab makes his last stand at the altar itself.

Adam Clarke uses the moment to explain the ancient right of sanctuary and its limits. "The altars were so sacred among all the people, that, in general, even the vilest wretch found safety, if he once reached the altar." But this very privilege bred abuse and "the perversion of public justice," until it became a settled maxim "that the guilty should be punished, should they even have taken refuge at the altars." Clarke grounds the exception in the Law: "God decreed that the presumptuous murderer who had taken refuge at the altar should be dragged thence, and put to death" (Exodus 21:14). Strikingly, he shows the same principle among the pagans, quoting Euripides: "If an unrighteous man, availing himself of the law, should claim the protection of the altar... I would drag him to justice, nor fear the wrath of the gods; for it is necessary that every wicked man should suffer for his crimes."

The witnesses read Joab's "I will die here" as both desperation and calculation. He may hope the altar will save him; or, failing that, that his death at the holy place will fasten odium on Solomon as a profaner of the sanctuary. Either way, the commentators agree, the Law is against him. The verse stages the confrontation the chapter has been building toward: a murderer clutching the horns of the altar, and a king who understands that the altar of the God of Israel "should give no protection to wilful murderers." That even the heathen, on Clarke's showing, felt the force of the same rule underscores its justice; the sacredness of the place cannot be pleaded to shelter the shedding of innocent blood. Joab's choice to die at the altar rather than come forth only sets the stage for the ruling of the next verse, where Solomon will bid Benaiah do the deed on the spot.
31:
Solomon's ruling meets Joab's defiance without flinching. Do as he hath said, and fall upon him, and bury him... that thou mayest take away the innocent blood, which Joab shed, from me, and from the house of my father. John Gill attends to the practical detail: Joab is to be slain "upon the spot" and then buried, not by the altar but "in his own sepulchre," for Benaiah, being of priestly rank, "could not be concerned in the burial of him," and such an office was beneath his dignity.

Matthew Henry draws out the theology that governs the command. The purpose Solomon names — the removal of guilt — is the key to the whole act: "By returning the innocent blood that had been shed upon the head of him that shed it, it was taken away from him and from the house of his father." Henry hears in this a solemn principle of magistracy: "the blood which is not required from the murderer will be required from the magistrate, at least there is danger lest it should." From it he presses a warning that reaches every household: "Those that would have their houses safe and built up must put away iniquity far from them." A throne, like a home, cannot be secure while unavenged blood cries against it.

The witnesses thus read the verse not as private revenge but as the discharge of a public debt. Joab's death is required by the innocent blood of Abner and Amasa, still resting on David's house until justice answers it; and Solomon acts as the magistrate whose duty is to see that cry satisfied. Even the arrangements for burial — swift execution, a private grave, no priestly hand defiled — express the same care that the deed be done in order and with reverence for holy things. What clemency had long deferred, and what a dying father had at last charged upon his son, the young king now performs: he lifts from his own head and his father's the guilt that would otherwise have polluted the reign, taking away the innocent blood so that the house of David may be built up in peace. The command to "fall upon him" is, on the witnesses' reading, an act of cleansing as much as of judgment.
32:
And the Lord shall return his blood upon his own head, who fell upon two men more righteous and better than he. John Gill reads the sentence as exact retribution — "by way of retaliation, blood for blood." The measure Joab dealt out is measured back to him; the God of justice returns the guilt to its source.

Matthew Henry weighs the aggravations that Solomon presses in naming the two victims. It heightened Joab's crime "that he fell upon two men more righteous and better than he, that had done him no wrong nor meant him any, and, had they lived, might probably have done David better service." Henry lays down the principle behind the enormity: "if the blood shed be not only innocent, but excellent, the life more valuable than common lives, the crime is the more heinous." And there was a further weight still: David "knew not of it, and yet the case was such that he would be suspected as privy to it; so that Joab endangered his prince's reputation in taking away the life of his rivals." The murders of Abner and Amasa had thus wounded not only their victims but the king whose cause they might have served.

The witnesses find in the verse the settled biblical law of bloodshed: that innocent blood does not lie silent, but cries until it is answered, and that God himself undertakes to return it upon the murderer's head. What David had deferred and Solomon now executes is presented not as the king's initiative merely but as the Lord's own act working through him — "the Lord shall return his blood." Joab had escaped human justice for a lifetime, sheltered by his power and his usefulness; but the verse declares that no length of impunity cancels the debt of murder. Solomon's stroke is the visible edge of an invisible judgment. The two captains whom Joab cut down treacherously, the better and more righteous men, are here vindicated at last, and the reader is left with the sober assurance the witnesses draw from the whole scene: that the God who keeps the reckoning will not let excellent blood, shed in peace under the guise of war, go forever unrequited.
33:
The sentence divides sharply into a curse and a blessing. Their blood shall therefore return upon the head of Joab, and upon the head of his seed for ever... but upon David, and upon his seed... shall there be peace for ever from the Lord. John Gill feels the weight of the entail of guilt: the blood returns "not only upon Joab, but upon his posterity as long as there would be any," so that "Joab's death would not be a sufficient satisfaction, but the punishment of his murders would be continued to his offspring."

Matthew Henry holds the two halves together as the fruit of one act of justice. On the house of Joab the crime brings "guilt, and shame, and a curse," so that his descendants, "instead of deriving honour... from his heroic actions," fared the worse for his villainous ones — "the seed of such evil doers shall never be renowned." But upon David's house the same justice secures "peace for ever from the Lord." Henry reads Solomon's words as both prayer and confidence: "Now that justice is done, and the cry of blood is satisfied, the government will prosper." He gathers it into a single radiant phrase — "thus righteousness and peace kiss each other" — and cites the proverb that grounds it: "Take away the wicked from before the king, and his throne shall be established in righteousness" (Proverbs 25:5).

The witnesses land the verse on its theological centre: peace is not bought by indulgence of evil but by the removal of it. The same stroke that curses the murderer's line blesses the king's, because both flow from the vindication of innocent blood. Henry is careful to trace the peace to its true author — "It shall be peace from the Lord, and peace for ever from the Lord" — and closes with a benediction that lifts the political settlement into the gospel: "The Lord of peace himself give us that peace which is everlasting." So the company reads Solomon's justice as a parable of a deeper order: that a throne, a house, a soul, finds lasting peace only where guilt is answered and the wicked are put away, and that such peace, when it comes, is always the gift of the Lord who alone can give it "for ever."
34:
So Benaiah the son of Jehoiada went up, and fell upon him, and slew him: and he was buried in his own house in the wilderness. John Gill sets the deed at "the great high place" of Gibeon, and Adam Clarke notes that "it appears he slew him at the very altar." Clarke pauses over the pathos and the justice together: Joab "must have been both old and infirm at this time," yet "now he bleeds for Abner, he bleeds for Amasa, and he bleeds for Uriah." The old general "had done the state much service, and they knew it. But he was a murderer, and vengeance would not suffer such to live."

Matthew Henry marks the manner of it. Benaiah "slew him with the solemnity, no doubt, of a public execution," and Joab "was buried in his own house in the wilderness, privately, like a criminal, not pompously, like a soldier." The warrior who had led Israel's armies goes to a quiet grave under the shadow of his crimes; yet Henry adds a restraining word, that "no indignity was done to his dead body," for "it is not for man to lay the iniquity upon the bones, whatever God does."

It is Henry who lifts the scene to its gospel height. Joab had grasped the horns of the altar and found no protection there; and from that failure Henry turns to the true refuge: "Those who, by a lively faith, take hold on Christ and his righteousness, with a resolution, if they perish, to perish there, shall find in him a more powerful protection than Joab found at the horns of the altar." The witnesses thus read the death of Joab as both the end of a long-deferred justice and a dark foil to the gospel. The altar of the old covenant could not shelter a wilful murderer; but the Christ whom that altar prefigured is a sanctuary that never fails the guilty who flee to him in faith. Where Joab's grasp on the horns availed nothing, the sinner's grasp on the crucified avails everything — and the very verse that records the fall of Israel's greatest captain becomes, in the witnesses' hands, an invitation to a better and surer refuge than any earthly altar could give.
35:
The vacancies left by justice are filled at once. The king put Benaiah the son of Jehoiada in his room over the host: and Zadok the priest did the king put in the room of Abiathar. John Gill notes the promotion of Benaiah "from being captain of his bodyguard to be general of the army," while Keil and Delitzsch record both appointments plainly — Benaiah as commander-in-chief in Joab's place, Zadok as priest in Abiathar's.

Matthew Henry reads the double preferment as the reward of fidelity and the fulfilment of prophecy. Benaiah and Zadok were "two faithful friends to Solomon and his government," and in Zadok's elevation "was fulfilled the word of God, when he threatened to cut off the house of Eli," saying, "I will raise me up a faithful priest, and will build him a sure house" (1 Samuel 2:35). Henry presses the encouraging truth beneath the personnel change: "Though sacred offices may be disgraced, they shall not be destroyed, by the mal-administration of those that are entrusted with them, nor shall God's work ever stand still for want of hands to carry it on." He notes, too, that Solomon "exercised this power with equity, for the ancient right was in Zadok, he being of the family of Eleazar, whereas Eli and his house were of Ithamar."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a curious observation from the later lists: while Benaiah succeeds to Joab's command over the host, "in the Hebrew text there is no mention of any successor to his command over the body-guard" (1 Kings 4:2–6). The witnesses read the verse as the quiet consolidation of the new reign: the throne cleared of its enemies is now staffed with faithful men, the priesthood restored to its rightful line, and the promise once spoken against Eli's house brought to its appointed end. What began as a season of executions issues in a settled order — a loyal general, a faithful priest — so that the kingdom "established greatly" of verse 12 is seen taking visible shape in the men who now stand nearest the king.
36:
The last of David's charges comes due, and with it the chapter's sharpest disagreement over Solomon's justice. Build thee a house in Jerusalem, and dwell there, and go not forth thence any whither. John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch supply the setting: Shimei is summoned from Bahurim, his home east of the city (2 Samuel 16:5), and forbidden ever to cross the brook Kidron on pain of death — the valley marking the eastern boundary, precisely the way back to his own people. Adam Clarke reads the terms as leniency itself: Solomon "gave him the whole city for a prison, and this certainly could have reduced him to no hardships." Matthew Henry agrees the test was gentle — "he has his life upon easy terms: he shall live if he will but be content to live at Jerusalem" — and calls it "a fair trial of his obedience, and such a test of his loyalty as he had no reason to complain of."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the opposite reading and states the debate at its plainest. The command "is in itself a reasonable precaution against treason, in one already powerful and of doubtful fidelity," designed to keep him from his power-base at Bahurim. "But it is difficult, in face of David's charge, to doubt that it was in some degree intended as a snare; and this view is confirmed by Solomon's words" in the later reckoning, which "refer back to the old offence of Shimei against David." Ellicott's verdict is careful and severe: "Legally the execution was justifiable, and it may have been politic; but it cannot stand examination on the ground of equity or generosity." He adds that it is "probably related here by anticipation."

So the witnesses part company. To Clarke and Henry the arrangement is mercy — a capital enemy granted his life on the easiest of conditions, and destroyed only when he freely breaks them. To Ellicott it is a baited trap, reasonable on its surface but designed to bring an old grudge to its long-intended end. The verse leaves both readings open, and the chapter will not finally close the question: whether Solomon offered Shimei a fair covenant of life, or set before him a boundary he was always expected, in time, to cross.
37:
The condition is spelled out with an oath's precision. On the day thou goest out, and passest over the brook Kidron, thou shalt know for certain that thou shalt surely die: thy blood shall be upon thine own head. John Gill notes why the Kidron is singled out: it "lay in his way to Bahurim, his native place," which he "must cross" to return home — the one direction Shimei would most be tempted to take, "through business, or a desire to see it again."

Adam Clarke reads the king's motive as prudent watchfulness. "Solomon knew that Shimei was a seditious man, and he chose to keep him under his own eye; for such a man at large, in favorable circumstances, might do much evil. His bitter revilings of David were a sufficient proof." The clause thy blood shall be upon thine own head Clarke glosses with care: "if thou disobey my orders thou shalt certainly be slain, and then thou shalt be considered as a self-murderer; thou alone shalt be answerable for thy own death." The terms are so plain that any breach would be Shimei's own doing, not the king's.

Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the narrative forward, note that Shimei "vowed obedience, and that on oath," and "kept his word a considerable time." The witnesses thus frame the arrangement as a covenant of life sealed by warning and consent. Whatever one makes of Solomon's deeper intent — the previous verse left that in dispute — this verse fixes the responsibility squarely on Shimei: the boundary is named, the penalty is named, the self-incurred guilt is named, and Shimei himself will swear to it. If he dies, it will be because he chose, with his eyes open, to cross a line he had been solemnly told was the line between life and death.
