The commentators who defend Solomon lean on exactly this clarity; even those who suspect a snare must grant that the trap, if trap it was, was set in the full light of day, its every consequence declared beforehand. The blood, Clarke insists, would rest where the verse places it — upon Shimei's own head.
38:
Shimei accepts the terms with apparent gladness. And Shimei said unto the king, The saying is good: as my lord the king hath said, so will thy servant do. John Gill hears real relief in the answer. Summoned by the son of the man he had cursed, and knowing "how he had used his father," and "hearing of several traitors being put to death, he expected this would have been his case." Instead of death he is merely required "to leave his habitation in the country, and come and live at Jerusalem, a pleasant and delightful city, and the metropolis of the nation" — a sentence "very agreeable to him."

Keil and Delitzsch add that Shimei's acceptance was more than a word of courtesy: "Shimei vowed obedience, and that on oath," and "he kept his word a considerable time," dwelling quietly in Jerusalem for three years before the crisis came. The commentators note the significance of that oath: the arrangement is now sealed on both sides, the king's warning met by the subject's sworn consent.

The witnesses read the moment as the closing of a bargain that Shimei himself pronounces "good." His own lips confess the terms fair; his own oath binds him to keep them. This matters for the debate that has run through the Shimei narrative, for it means that whatever verdict one reaches on Solomon's motive, Shimei entered the covenant willingly and even thankfully, judging it a mercy that he should live at all. Gill's picture of a relieved man, expecting a traitor's death and receiving instead a comfortable exile within the walls of the holy city, throws the later tragedy into relief: the fault, when it comes, will lie not in the terms he gladly took but in his own failure to keep the oath he freely swore. For now the scene is peaceable — an old enemy of the house of David spared, settled in Jerusalem, and pledged by his own word to the one condition on which his life depends. Gill notes the reversal in Shimei’s fortunes: the man who had flung stones and curses at a fleeing king is now the grateful pensioner of that king’s son, exchanging his country holding at Bahurim for a dwelling in the metropolis. The witnesses let the irony stand without comment, content to observe that Shimei is here at his safest, bound by an oath he judges good, and that only his own hand can undo the mercy he has just received.
39:
After three years the peace is broken by a small occasion. At the end of three years... two of the servants of Shimei ran away unto Achish son of Maachah king of Gath. And they told Shimei, saying, Behold, thy servants be in Gath. John Gill marks only the lapse of time — the three years Shimei "had dwelt at Jerusalem" in quiet obedience. Keil and Delitzsch fill in the circumstance: two slaves fled to Gath, "to king Achish, with whom David had also sought and found refuge" (1 Samuel 27:2), and when Shimei learned of it "he started for Gath as soon as he knew this, and fetched them back."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to a chronological puzzle in the name. This "Achish son of Maachah" cannot easily be the same "Achish son of Maoch, king of Gath" who had sheltered David a generation before (1 Samuel 27:2); "chronology makes it most unlikely that the same person should here be referred to," and Ellicott suggests "the name may have been hereditary" in the royal house of Gath. The detail, small as it is, anchors the story in a real and traceable history.

The witnesses read the verse as the quiet turning of the trap — whether a trap of Solomon's setting or of Shimei's own careless heart. For three years the boundary had held; now a trivial matter, the flight of two servants toward the very city where David once hid, draws Shimei's eye beyond the wall. There is a certain irony the commentators feel without labouring: the man who had once cursed the fleeing David is now tempted to flee, in his turn, after runaway property of his own, toward the old Philistine refuge. Nothing in the errand is wicked in itself — a master seeking his slaves — and yet it leads straight to the forbidden crossing. The scene is poised on the edge of ruin, and the witnesses let the reader see how the smallest concern of daily life, a couple of absconded servants, can become the occasion by which a man forfeits everything he had sworn to keep. The three quiet years are over; the test has come.
40:
Shimei's own act undoes him. And Shimei arose, and saddled his ass, and went to Gath to Achish to seek his servants... and brought his servants from Gath. John Gill probes the tangle of motives that led him across the fatal line: "through the passion he was in with his servants, and his hurry to get them home, and the covetous disposition which prevailed on him, he might forget, or be tempted to neglect, the prohibition." Perhaps he thought Solomon had forgotten it, or could come and go unnoticed, or would not punish "so small a fault." Yet Gill sees a deeper hand at work: "so it was ordered by the providence of God, leaving him to his own lust, and the temptations of Satan, that he might suffer just punishment for cursing David."

Adam Clarke can only marvel at the folly: "It is astonishing that with his eyes wide open he would thus run into the jaws of death." Matthew Henry draws the pastoral moral through the words of Bishop Hall: "Seeking his servants, he lost himself; those earthly things either are, or should be, our servants. How commonly do we see men run out of the bounds set by God's law, to hunt after them, till their souls incur a fearful judgment!"

The witnesses converge on a single, searching lesson. What destroys Shimei is not a grand rebellion but a small, ordinary greed — a couple of runaway slaves and a master too eager to recover his property to remember the terms of his life. The very care for earthly things that ought to be his servants makes him their slave, and he crosses the line he had sworn never to cross. Gill's twin reading holds the whole matter: Shimei acts freely, from his own passion and covetousness, and yet the God who governs all things leaves him to that lust so that an old, unpunished sin may at last find its reckoning. The commentators do not excuse Solomon by this providence, nor do they excuse Shimei by his ordinary motive; they see rather how a life may be forfeited in the pursuit of a trifle, and how the sins men think forgotten wait patiently for the day when their own folly delivers them up to judgment.
41:
The forbidden journey does not go unobserved. And it was told Solomon that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath, and was come again. John Gill supposes the report reached the king "by the spies he set to watch and observe his motions, or by some others who had seen him go out and return, and knew that it was contrary to the king's orders." Either way, the crossing of the brook Kidron, done perhaps in the hope of secrecy, is at once brought to the throne.

Matthew Henry marks this as the moment the king "takes the forfeiture." The whole arrangement had been, in effect, a bond under penalty of death, and now "information is given him that Shimei has transgressed." Henry had already read Shimei's breach as the very thing Solomon expected, and as a righteous providence: "God was righteous in suffering him to do it, that he might now suffer for his old sins." The report is thus the hinge on which the long-suspended sentence at last swings shut.

The witnesses find in the brief notice a sober lesson about the reach of a king's eye — and, beyond it, of a greater King's. Shimei may have imagined that a quiet errand to Gath and back, undertaken in the press of private business, would escape notice; but nothing done outside the appointed bounds is hidden from the throne. Gill's picture of watchers set to observe his motions, and Henry's of a forfeiture now claimed, together frame the truth the chapter has been pressing since David's deathbed: that guilt long carried is not guilt escaped, and that the day of reckoning waits only for the sinner's own act to bring it on. Shimei had sworn to keep within the city; he has broken his oath and returned, thinking the matter closed; and the plain words "it was told Solomon" announce that it is not closed at all, but opened at last to judgment. The test set three years before has been failed; and where Shimei imagined the matter closed, the plain words announce that his own act has laid it open before the throne. The king now knows what Shimei had hoped no one would see, and the sentence so long suspended has only to be spoken.
42:
Summoned again before the king, Shimei is confronted first with the broken oath. Did I not make thee to swear by the Lord, and protested unto thee... that thou shalt surely die? and thou saidst unto me, The word that I have heard is good. John Gill names the offence precisely: by not keeping an oath "made by him, and in his presence, and in which he was appealed to," Shimei "was guilty of perjury." The crime is not merely disobedience but the profaning of the Lord's name he had invoked.

Matthew Henry lays out the double contempt in the charge. Solomon shows "that he had put a great contempt upon the authority and wrath both of God and the king, that he had broken the oath of the Lord and disobeyed the commandment of his prince." Henry reads Shimei's calculation as an aggravation rather than an excuse: "Had he represented to Solomon the urgency of the occasion, and begged leave to go, perhaps Solomon might have given him leave; but to presume either upon his ignorance or his connivance was to affront him in the highest degree." The quiet slipping out and back, trusting the king would not notice or not care, was itself an insult to the throne.

Keil and Delitzsch note that the king now turns from the broken oath to the deeper guilt behind it, reminding Shimei "of the evil which he had done to his father." The witnesses read the verse as the formal arraignment: Solomon does not strike in passion but sets the sworn condition beside the plain fact of its breach, and lets Shimei's own remembered words — "The word that I have heard is good" — condemn him. The man had judged the terms good and sworn to keep them; he has broken them and can plead neither ignorance of the boundary nor doubt of the penalty. The commentators, whatever their view of Solomon's original intent, agree that on the face of it the case is unanswerable: an oath sworn by the Lord, a commandment plainly given, a boundary knowingly crossed. Shimei stands convicted out of his own mouth, and the king has only to pass from the charge to the sentence.
43:
The charge is pressed home as a question that admits no answer. Why then hast thou not kept the oath of the Lord, and the commandment that I have charged thee with? John Gill fixes on the sacred character of what was broken: it was "the oath of the Lord," sworn "in his presence," so that "by not keeping it" Shimei "was guilty of perjury." The offence is at once against the king's commandment and against the God by whom the oath was sworn.

Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment gathers the king's whole speech, hear the two counts joined in this single sentence — "Why hast thou not kept the oath of Jehovah (the oath sworn by Jehovah)... and the commandment that I have charged thee with?" — before Solomon passes on to the older crime that lay behind the recent breach. The question is rhetorical: there is no urgency of runaway servants that could justify the profaning of an oath taken in the name of the Lord.

The witnesses read the verse as the exposure of Shimei's silence. He had, by his own confession, judged the terms good and sworn to keep them; now, faced with the plain question why he has not, he has nothing to say. The commentators note how the double naming — the oath of the Lord and the commandment of the king — binds the human and the divine together, so that Shimei's fault cannot be reduced to a mere technical trespass against a royal boundary. To break the commandment was to disobey the prince; to break the oath was to despise the God whose name he had called upon to seal it. Even those among the witnesses who suspect Solomon's motives grant the justice of the charge on its own terms: Shimei stands accused not of a scruple but of perjury, and the question the king puts to him is one his conscience cannot meet. The plain sense of the verse is that a man who lightly breaks a vow made to God has no defence, and the interrogation moves inexorably toward the sentence that the next verses will pronounce.
44:
Behind the broken oath Solomon reaches back to the old wound. Thou knowest all the wickedness which thine heart is privy to, that thou didst to David my father: therefore the Lord shall return thy wickedness upon thine own head. John Gill reads this not as a second ground of death but as God's providence bringing an old sin to reckoning through a new one — Solomon reminds Shimei of "his former sins," and observes "the providence of God in suffering him to fall into others, that justice might take place upon him for them also."

Keil and Delitzsch render the phrase with force: "Thou knowest all the evil, which thy heart knoweth (i.e., which thy conscience must tell thee); and now Jehovah returns the evil upon thy head" — the death he deserved "for blaspheming the anointed of the Lord" when he cursed the fleeing David (2 Samuel 16:5). Matthew Henry presses the appeal to conscience most searchingly. "There was no need to examine witnesses for the proof of the fact, his own conscience was instead of a thousand witnesses." Others had seen Shimei curse David, but "Shimei himself knew of the wicked principles of hatred and malice against David which he displayed in cursing him, and that his submission was but feigned and forced." Henry draws the awful inference: "if the heart be privy to it, God is greater than the heart, and knoweth all things" (1 John 3:20).

The witnesses thus find in the verse the meeting of memory, conscience, and divine justice. The curse Shimei had flung at David in his day of affliction, and the grudging submission that had covered but not cured his malice, are now returned upon his own head. Charles Ellicott, who had suspected the whole arrangement of being a snare, concedes that Solomon's own words "refer back to the old offence of Shimei against David" — the very point on which the plainer reading rests. Whatever the debate over Solomon's motive, the commentators agree on the theology: a man's own heart is the surest witness against him, and the sin he thought long buried, known fully to God if to no one else, waits to be requited. Shimei is condemned less by the king's spies than by his own conscience, and the God who "knoweth all things" now returns his wickedness where it belongs.
45:
Over against the doomed man Solomon sets a blessing on his own house. And king Solomon shall be blessed, and the throne of David shall be established before the Lord for ever. John Gill reads the blessing concretely — "a long and peaceable reign, and large dominions, notwithstanding all the attempts to make him unhappy." Keil and Delitzsch tie the blessing to the justice just done: the king and throne are blessed and established "because the king does justice."

Adam Clarke, who has been the fiercest critic of the Shimei affair, here states the principle that Solomon acts upon, whether or not he endorses its application: the king "seems to think that, while such bad men remained unpunished, the nation could not prosper; that it was an act of justice which God required him to perform, in order to the establishment and perpetuity of his throne." Matthew Henry reads the contrast most boldly. Solomon "blessed himself and his government... notwithstanding Shimei's impotent curses, which perhaps, in fury and despair, he now vented freely." Henry hears beneath it the echo of David's own experience — "Let them curse, but bless thou" — and the promise that "the throne of David shall be established, by taking away those that would undermine it."

From this the witnesses rise to the verse's largest note. Henry turns it into a comfort for the whole church against all her enemies: "how much soever they rage, it is a vain thing they imagine. Christ's throne is established, and they cannot shake it." The blessing pronounced on Solomon's throne "before the Lord for ever" points beyond the son of David to the greater Son, whose kingdom the raging of the wicked can no more overthrow than Shimei's curses could unseat Solomon. The commentators thus read the verse in two registers at once: on its surface, a king declaring that justice done on a perjured enemy secures peace to his reign; and beneath it, a promise that the throne of David, established "before the Lord for ever," will outlast every curse hurled against it — a stability that, for the witnesses, finds its true and final ground in Christ. The doom of Shimei and the blessing of the throne are one act, for the same justice that removes the undermining enemy establishes the house in peace.
46:
The chapter closes with a sentence executed and a kingdom secured. So the king commanded Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; which went out, and fell upon him, that he died. And the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon. John Gill notes Benaiah took Shimei to "the proper place of execution," it being unfitting to slay him before the king. Keil and Delitzsch pronounce the punishment "also just": Solomon had "put Shimei's life in his own hand" by the terms of his confinement, and Shimei's oath-breaking "was a crime for which he had no excuse," the pleas urged in his favour — the value of the runaway slaves, his right to reclaim them — having "no force at all," since he might have sought the king's leave instead of lightly breaking his vow.

Here Adam Clarke reopens the chapter's deepest question, carrying forward the argument he began at David's charge. Drawing on Kennicott, he contends that David's dying instruction has been misread: as the negative in "hold him not guiltless" is meant to be repeated in the following clause, so David commanded that Shimei's "hoar head" be brought not "down to the grave with blood." Clarke marshals a string of Hebrew parallels where an unexpressed negative carries over — "The ungodly shall not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous" (Psalm 1:5); the needy shall not always be forgotten, nor the expectation of the poor perish (Psalm 9:18); "rebuke me not... neither chasten me" (Psalm 38:1); and others in Proverbs 24:12 and Isaiah 13:22. He clinches it from the narrative itself: Solomon "did not kill Shimei" on David's charge, but confined him for years — behaviour fitting a command of restraint, not death. "This is the best mode of interpreting this text."

Against Clarke stand Gill, Keil, and Matthew Henry, who take the plain reading: David handed on an unfinished justice, and Shimei dies for breaking his own oath, not for the old curse alone. Henry lifts the ending to its christological height. "All judgment is committed to the Lord Jesus, and, though he be King of peace, he will be found a King of righteousness"; Solomon's word over his enemies — "Bring them forth, and slay them before me" — foreshadows the final reckoning of Christ with those who would not have him reign. So the witnesses close the chapter on its double note: a throne "established in the hand of Solomon" by the removal of every rival, and, behind it, "the type of the Redeemer's kingdom of peace and righteousness," whose establishment no curse can shake and whose justice, deferred but sure, will one day be complete.
